column2: Public Law 93-620 (1975) added about 185,000 acres to the Havasupai Indian Reservation in northern Arizona, and this draft EIS evaluates the Secretarial Land Use Plan developed by the Secretary of the Interior with the Havasupai Tribal Council to guide use of that land. The plan covers 20 topics including agriculture, grazing, residential areas, domestic and stock water, energy, roads and trails, fencing, revegetation, waste management, visitor use, and wildlife management. Specific actions include four designated residential areas (Pasture Wash, south of Hualapai Hilltop, Moqui Tank, and eas…

column2: The Havasupai Tribe is the primary affected community, with about 425 enrolled members in roughly 60 families and a reservation population that fluctuated around 281–316 in 1976–1977, almost all living in Havasu Canyon. The tribal economy depends heavily on tourist packing with horses, wage and salary work (much of it federally funded), a small cattle herd, and limited gardening, with high unemployment, fragmented land assignments, and 71 percent of personal income coming from federal sources. Adjacent and related parties include the Hualapai Tribe, Grand Canyon National Park, Kaibab National…

The main alternative considered is No Action, which would leave water sources unreliable, forgo income gains, and allow continued overgrazing by uncontrolled horses. Within individual plan components, alternatives include several ways of supplying domestic water (atmospheric catchment, wells, tapping rim aquifers, piping from Havasu Creek, or treating stock-tank water), different energy options (local generators, an extended commercial power line, or wind and solar units), grazing strategies ranging from mostly cattle to a mixed horse-and-cattle herd, sewage disposal methods at Havasu Campgro…

Expected impacts include localized soil erosion on silty alluvial soils, dust and minor air pollution from cleared agricultural land and improved roads, construction noise, and permanent loss of sagebrush, juniper-pinyon, and saltbush vegetation where cleared, with possible reductions in canyon spring flows if aquifers are tapped. Wildlife concerns include disturbance of bighorn sheep on Great Thumb Mesa, fence barriers to deer and antelope, displacement of sagebrush-associated species, increased hunting pressure, and continued degradation of Cataract Canyon bottomlands and Havasu Creek water…

Comments on the draft were requested from federal agencies (USDA, BLM, Corps of Engineers, EPA, National Park Service, Fish and Wildlife Service, and others), the Arizona State Historic Preservation Officer, the Arizona State Museum, the State Clearinghouse, the Havasupai and Hualapai Tribes, and the University of Arizona, and Public Law 93-620 requires public review, hearings, and transmittal of transcripts to Congress at least 90 days before implementation.
